Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.

Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.

- Action model: UP/DOWN moves player vertically, RIGHT extends trail. Need to figure out how to "collect" targets.

end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

After 3 LEFT actions:

All three targets are still on the trail. The green target is at cols 18-21, which is within the trail's range (16-22). The blue target is at cols 24-27, which is outside the trail's range (16-22). Wait, that's strange. The blue target is at cols 24-27, but the trail is at cols 16-22. So the blue target is NOT on the trail.
But the blue target is still at rows 19-22, which is the trail's row. So the blue target is on the trail's row but not on the trail's column range.
Hmm, let me look at the image. The image shows: player, green, blue, red from left to right. The green target is adjacent to the player, and the blue and red targets are to the right of the green target. The trail is short (cols 16-22), and the green target is at cols 18-21, which is on the trail. The blue target is at cols 24-27, which is just to the right of the trail. The red target is at cols 30-33, which is further to the right.
So the blue and red targets are NOT on the trail, but they're still on the trail's row. They're "waiting" to be collected again.
This gives me an idea. If I extend the trail with RIGHT, the trail will extend past the blue and red targets' columns, and they will be "re-collected" (they'll snap to the trail's row and shift left by 6 cols).
But wait, the blue and red targets are already on the trail's row (rows 19-22). So they don't need to snap. They just need to shift left by 6 cols when the trail extends past their columns.
Let me try extending the trail with RIGHT and see what happens.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the order is: player, green (on trail), blue (off trail), red (off trail). The panel shows: pink, red, green, blue.
If I extend the trail with RIGHT, the trail will extend past the blue target's column (24-27), and the blue target will shift left by 6 cols to cols 18-21. But the green target is already at cols 18-21. So the blue target would overlap with the green target.
Hmm, that doesn't seem right. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, when the trail extends past a target's column, the target shifts left by 6 cols. So if the trail extends past the blue target's column (24-27), the blue target would shift left by 6 cols to cols 18-21. But the green target is already at cols 18-21. So the blue target would overlap with the green target.
But wait, the green target is on the trail, and the blue target is off the trail. When the trail extends, the green target shifts left by 6 cols (because it's on the trail), and the blue target also shifts left by 6 cols (because the trail extends past its column). So both targets shift left by 6 cols, and they would still overlap.
This doesn't seem right. Let me try extending the trail with RIGHT and see what happens.
Actually, I think the key insight is that when the trail retracts, the targets on the trail shift left by 6 cols, but the targets off the trail don't shift. So after 3 LEFT actions:
- Green: cols 18-21 (on trail, shifted left 18 cols total)
- Blue: cols 24-27 (off trail, shifted left 12 cols total)
- Red: cols 30-33 (off trail, shifted left 6 cols total)
Wait, that doesn't match. Let me re-examine.

- Blue: cols 30-33
- Red: cols 36-39

But the actual result shows:

- Blue: cols 24-27

So the targets didn't shift left by 6 cols each time. They only shifted left once (by 6 cols total), not 3 times (by 18 cols total).
Wait, let me re-examine. Before the 3 LEFT actions, the state was:
- Green: cols 24-27
- Blue: cols 30-33

After 3 LEFT actions, the state is:

So the targets shifted left by 6 cols total (not 18 cols). The trail retracted by 18 cols (from 40 to 22).
This suggests that the targets only shift left by 6 cols when the trail retracts past their column. Once the trail is no longer past their column, they don't shift anymore.
So the mechanic is:
- When the trail retracts, targets on the trail shift left by 6 cols.
- When the trail extends, targets that the trail passes over shift left by 6 cols.
- Targets only shift once per trail extension/retraction cycle.
This explains why the targets only shifted left by 6 cols total, even though I pressed LEFT 3 times.

So the green target shifted left 4 times (once when collected, and 3 times when the trail retracted). The blue target shifted left 3 times (once when collected, and 2 times when the trail retracted). The red target shifted left 2 times (once when collected, and 1 time when the trail retracted).
Wait, let me re-examine. The blue target was collected when the trail extended to cols 16-46. The blue target was at cols 42-45, and it shifted left by 6 cols to cols 36-39. Then when I pressed LEFT, the blue target shifted left by 6 cols to cols 30-33. Hmm, this is confusing. Let me re-examine the sequence of events.
Actually, I think the issue is that the targets only shift when the trail's end passes their column. When the trail retracts from cols 16-40 to cols 16-34 (1 LEFT), the trail's end moves from col 40 to col 34. The blue target is at cols 30-33, which is within the trail's range (16-34). When the trail retracts from cols 16-28 to cols 16-22 (3 LEFT), the trail's end moves from col 28 to col 22. The blue target is at cols 18-21, which is within the trail's range (16-22). So the blue target shifts left by 6 cols to cols 12-15.

Let me think about this differently. Maybe the targets only shift when the trail extends or retracts past their column. sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: After 3 LEFT, trail retracted to cols 16-22. Targets shifted left 6 cols each: green (18-21, on trail), blue (24-27, off trail), red (30-33, off trail). Targets shift when trail end passes their column.
Goal model: Need order red, green, blue. Currently green, blue, red. Need to figure out rearrangement mechanic.
Plan: Extend trail RIGHT to re-collect blue and red targets, observe what happens when they shift into green's position.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Extend trail RIGHT to re-collect blue and red targets
result = action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
print("Result:", last_action_result)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Check all targets
for color, name in [('R', 'red'), ('b', 'blue'), ('N', 'green')]:
    targets = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == color and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
    for t in targets:
        rows = [p[0] for p in t['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in t['boundary']]
        print(f"{name}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check trail
trail_elements = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] in ['g', 'w'] and 15 <= n['boundary'][0][1] <= 50 and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
if trail_elements:
    max_col = max([max(p[1] for p in t['boundary']) for t in trail_elements])
    min_col = min([min(p[1] for p in t['boundary']) for t in trail_elements])
    trail_rows = set()
    for t in trail_elements:
        for p in t['boundary']:
            trail_rows.add(p[0])
    print(f"Trail: cols {min_col}-{max_col}, rows: {sorted(trail_rows)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Extend trail RIGHT to re-collect blue and red targets
result = action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
print("Result:", last_action_result)

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']

# Check all targets
for color, name in [('R', 'red'), ('b', 'blue'), ('N', 'green')]:
    targets = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == color and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
    for t in targets:
        rows = [p[0] for p in t['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in t['boundary']]
        print(f"{name}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check trail
trail_elements = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] in ['g', 'w'] and 15 <= n['boundary'][0][1] <= 50 and n['boundary'][0][0] < 50]
if trail_elements:
    max_col = max([max(p[1] for p in t['boundary']) for t in trail_elements])
    min_col = min([min(p[1] for p in t['boundary']) for t in trail_elements])
    trail_rows = set()
    for t in trail_elements:
        for p in t['boundary']:
            trail_rows.add(p[0])
    print(f"Trail: cols {min_col}-{max_col}, rows: {sorted(trail_rows)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
